quiz_questions.question_text: A $6,500 Traditional IRA contribution in the 22% bracket saves how much in current-year taxes?
quiz_questions.explanation: $6,500 multiplied by the 22% marginal rate = $1,430 in tax savings. Traditional IRA contributions reduce taxable income dollar-for-dollar.

quiz_questions.question_text: A Roth IRA is generally better when you expect your tax rate in retirement to be:

options.text: Lower than your current rate
Higher than your current rate — pay tax now at the lower rate to avoid higher taxes later
The same as your current rate
It never matters — Roth and Traditional are identical

quiz_questions.explanation: If you expect higher taxes in retirement (due to pension income, rising rates, or RMDs), paying tax now at your current lower rate and letting the Roth grow tax-free saves money long term.

quiz_questions.question_text: Net capital losses exceeding gains can offset ordinary income up to _____ per year.

options.text: Unlimited

quiz_questions.explanation: Up to $3,000 in net capital losses can be deducted against ordinary income each year. Any remaining losses carry forward to future years indefinitely.

quiz_questions.question_text: $10,000 contributed to a traditional 401(k) in the 22% bracket saves how much in current-year taxes?
quiz_questions.explanation: $10,000 multiplied by 0.22 = $2,200. Traditional 401(k) contributions reduce your taxable income in the year they are made.

quiz_questions.question_text: Municipal bond interest is generally:

options.text: Fully taxable at ordinary income rates
Exempt from federal income tax, and often exempt from state tax if the bond is from your state
Taxed at capital gains rates
Only partially exempt for high earners

quiz_questions.explanation: Municipal bond interest is exempt from federal income tax. If the bond is issued in your home state, it is usually exempt from state tax as well, making it especially attractive for high-bracket investors.

quiz_questions.question_text: The wash-sale rule prevents you from:

options.text: Selling stocks at a profit
Claiming a tax loss if you buy the same or substantially identical security within 30 days before or after the sale
Buying stocks in a tax-advantaged account
Donating stocks to charity

quiz_questions.explanation: The wash-sale rule disallows a capital loss deduction if you repurchase the same or substantially identical security within 30 days (before or after) the sale. The disallowed loss is added to the cost basis of the replacement shares.

quiz_questions.question_text: Bunching deductions means:

options.text: Always taking the standard deduction
Concentrating multiple years of deductible expenses into one year to exceed the standard deduction threshold, then taking the standard deduction in alternate years
Evenly spreading deductions across all years
Maximizing the number of tax credits claimed

quiz_questions.explanation: Bunching accelerates deductible expenses (charitable donations, medical expenses) into one tax year so they exceed the standard deduction, then takes the standard deduction in off years, yielding higher total deductions over the two-year cycle.

quiz_questions.question_text: Tax-deferred growth means an investment:

options.text: Never owes any tax
Grows without annual taxation on dividends and gains, allowing all money to compound; taxes are due upon withdrawal
Is taxed at a lower rate than ordinary income
Is only available inside Roth accounts

quiz_questions.explanation: Tax deferral allows the full investment to compound without annual drag from taxes. Over decades, this can result in significantly more wealth than a taxable account with the same return.

quiz_questions.question_text: An HSA (Health Savings Account) is called 'triple tax-advantaged' because:

options.text: You can contribute three times the normal limit
Contributions are tax-deductible, growth is tax-free, and qualified medical withdrawals are tax-free
It offers three different investment options
Three family members can use the same account

quiz_questions.explanation: HSAs provide three tax benefits: (1) pre-tax or tax-deductible contributions, (2) tax-free investment growth, and (3) tax-free withdrawals for qualified medical expenses. No other account offers all three.

quiz_questions.question_text: A Roth conversion from a Traditional IRA in the 22% bracket means you:

options.text: Pay no taxes on the conversion
Pay income tax at 22% on the converted amount now, but all future growth and withdrawals are tax-free
Pay capital gains tax on the conversion
Lose the converted money permanently

quiz_questions.explanation: A Roth conversion requires paying income tax on the converted amount at your current marginal rate. Once in the Roth, all future growth and qualified withdrawals are tax-free forever.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is 'tax-loss harvesting'?

options.text: A strategy of intentionally selling investments at a loss to offset capital gains and reduce your tax bill
A method of collecting tax refunds from previous years
Waiting until retirement to sell losing investments
A government program to help investors recover losses

quiz_questions.explanation: Tax-loss harvesting involves selling investments that have declined in value to realize capital losses. These losses offset capital gains and up to $3,000 of ordinary income annually.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'tax-deferred' mean?

options.text: Taxes are permanently forgiven
Taxes on investment growth are postponed until funds are withdrawn, allowing the full amount to compound over time
Taxes are paid in advance before investing
The investment is exempt from all forms of taxation

quiz_questions.explanation: Tax deferral delays taxation until withdrawal, typically in retirement. Traditional IRAs, 401(k)s, and annuities are common tax-deferred vehicles. The benefit is that more money stays invested and compounds.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is a 'Roth conversion'?

options.text: Moving money from a Roth IRA to a Traditional IRA
Transferring money from a Traditional IRA or 401(k) to a Roth IRA, paying income tax on the converted amount now so future growth and withdrawals are tax-free
Converting stock investments into bond investments within a Roth IRA
Closing a retirement account and receiving a cash payout

quiz_questions.explanation: A Roth conversion moves pre-tax retirement funds into a Roth IRA. You pay income tax on the amount converted, but all future growth and qualified withdrawals become permanently tax-free.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'deduction bunching' mean?

options.text: The strategy of concentrating deductible expenses into alternating years to exceed the standard deduction, maximizing total deductions over a two-year cycle
Filing taxes with multiple deductions at the same time
Combining all family members' deductions into one return
Taking every possible deduction regardless of eligibility

quiz_questions.explanation: Deduction bunching involves timing expenses (like charitable donations or medical procedures) to cluster them in one tax year. This way, you itemize when deductions exceed the standard deduction and take the standard deduction in other years.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is a 'donor-advised fund' (DAF)?

options.text: A government program that distributes funds to charities
A charitable giving account where you make a tax-deductible contribution now but recommend grants to charities over time
A savings account that earns interest for charity
A type of retirement account for nonprofit workers

quiz_questions.explanation: A DAF allows you to make a large tax-deductible contribution in a high-income year (useful for bunching), then distribute the funds to charities at your own pace over future years.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'income timing' mean for tax planning?

options.text: Choosing when to receive or recognize income to minimize taxes, such as deferring a bonus to a lower-income year
Tracking the exact date each paycheck is deposited
Filing your taxes as early as possible to receive a refund sooner
Working fewer hours to stay in a lower tax bracket

quiz_questions.explanation: Income timing involves strategically shifting when income is received (e.g., deferring a December bonus to January) to manage which tax year it falls in, potentially keeping you in a lower bracket.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the 'wash-sale rule'?

options.text: A rule preventing you from selling stocks during market downturns
An IRS rule that disallows a capital loss deduction if you purchase the same or substantially identical security within 30 days before or after the sale
A regulation requiring you to hold investments for at least one year
A restriction on how many trades you can make in a single day

quiz_questions.explanation: The wash-sale rule prevents taxpayers from selling a security at a loss and immediately repurchasing it just to claim the tax deduction. The 30-day window applies both before and after the sale date.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the 'tax-equivalent yield' of a municipal bond?

options.text: The yield a taxable bond must offer to match the after-tax return of a tax-exempt municipal bond, calculated as muni yield divided by (1 minus marginal tax rate)
The total interest earned on a municipal bond over its lifetime
The yield of a municipal bond after paying state taxes
The average yield of all bonds in a municipal bond fund

quiz_questions.explanation: Tax-equivalent yield = muni yield / (1 - marginal tax rate). A 3.5% muni bond for someone in the 32% bracket has a tax-equivalent yield of 3.5% / 0.68 = 5.15%, meaning they would need a 5.15% taxable bond to match.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'S-corp election' mean for tax purposes?

options.text: A business structure that allows income to pass through to the owner's personal return, with the ability to split income into salary (subject to FICA) and distributions (not subject to FICA)
A type of retirement account for small business owners
A special election to pay no taxes for the first year of business
A state-level business license classification

quiz_questions.explanation: An S-corp election allows business owners to split income between salary and distributions, potentially saving thousands in self-employment (FICA) tax since distributions are not subject to the 15.3% SE tax.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'tax optimization' mean?

options.text: Illegally hiding income from the IRS
The legal and strategic use of tax code provisions to minimize tax liability through deductions, timing, account selection, and planning
Paying extra taxes to avoid audits
Hiring the most expensive tax accountant available

quiz_questions.explanation: Tax optimization is the legal practice of structuring finances to minimize tax burden using provisions in the tax code. This includes maximizing deductions, choosing the right accounts, timing income, and strategic charitable giving.

quiz_questions.question_text: Alex earns $85,000 and expects to be in the 12% bracket during retirement. He contributes $6,500 to a Traditional IRA at his current 22% marginal rate. What is his net lifetime tax savings per contribution compared to a Roth IRA?

options.text: $650 — he deducts at 22% now ($1,430) and pays 12% later ($780), netting $650
$0 — both produce the same result

quiz_questions.explanation: Traditional IRA: save $6,500 x 0.22 = $1,430 now, pay $6,500 x 0.12 = $780 in retirement. Net savings = $1,430 - $780 = $650. When your current rate is higher than your expected retirement rate, Traditional wins.

quiz_questions.question_text: Beth has a $10,000 stock gain and a $4,000 loss in her taxable brokerage account. She is in the 15% long-term capital gains bracket. How much does tax-loss harvesting save her?
$100,000 invested at 7% for 20 years: one account is tax-deferred, the other is taxable and loses approximately 1% annually to taxes (6% effective growth). What is the approximate difference?

options.text: About $67,000 more in the tax-deferred account
About $10,000 more in the tax-deferred account
About $150,000 more in the tax-deferred account
No difference — taxes are the same either way

quiz_questions.explanation: Tax-deferred at 7% for 20 years: $100,000 x 1.07^20 = ~$387,000. Taxable at 6% effective: $100,000 x 1.06^20 = ~$321,000. Difference = ~$66,000. Tax deferral provides more efficient compounding.

quiz_questions.question_text: A couple earns $150,000 combined and donates $8,000/year to charity. The 2024 standard deduction for MFJ is $29,200. They have $15,000 in other itemized deductions. Should they bunch two years of donations into one year?

options.text: No — their deductions already exceed the standard deduction every year
Yes — bunching gives them $31,000 in Year 1 (itemize) and $15,000 in Year 2 (standard), totaling $60,200 in deductions vs. $46,000 spread evenly
No — bunching is only for single filers
Yes — but the savings are negligible

quiz_questions.explanation: Even spread: $15,000 + $8,000 = $23,000/year (below $29,200), so they take standard both years = $58,400 total. Bunched: Year 1: $15,000 + $16,000 = $31,000 (itemize); Year 2: $15,000 (standard $29,200). Total = $60,200. Extra $1,800 in deductions.

quiz_questions.question_text: Carlos earns $150,000 as a sole proprietor. He considers electing S-corp status and paying himself a $90,000 salary. How much would he save annually in self-employment tax on the $60,000 in distributions?
quiz_questions.explanation: As sole proprietor, SE tax on $60,000 = approximately $60,000 x 0.9235 x 0.153 = $8,478. As S-corp, distributions of $60,000 are not subject to FICA. Approximate savings = ~$9,180 per year.

quiz_questions.question_text: Diana is in the 32% bracket and considering a 3.5% municipal bond vs. a 5% corporate bond. Which provides higher after-tax yield?

options.text: The corporate bond at 5%
The municipal bond — its tax-equivalent yield is 5.15%, beating the corporate bond's 3.4% after-tax yield
They produce the same after-tax return
Cannot be determined without knowing the bond's maturity

quiz_questions.explanation: Muni tax-equivalent yield = 3.5% / (1 - 0.32) = 5.15%. Corporate after-tax yield = 5% x (1 - 0.32) = 3.4%. The muni bond's 5.15% equivalent yield exceeds the corporate bond's 3.4% after-tax return.

quiz_questions.question_text: Eric plans to convert $50,000 from his Traditional IRA to a Roth during a sabbatical year when his income drops to $20,000 (12% bracket) instead of his usual $90,000 (22% bracket). How much does the lower-bracket conversion save compared to converting in a normal year?
quiz_questions.explanation: At 22%: conversion tax = $50,000 x 0.22 = $11,000. At 12%: conversion tax = $50,000 x 0.12 = $6,000. Savings = $11,000 - $6,000 = $5,000. Low-income years are ideal for Roth conversions.

quiz_questions.question_text: Fiona contributes $3,850 to her HSA, invests it, and it grows to $8,000 over 10 years. She withdraws $8,000 for qualified medical expenses. If she is in the 24% bracket, what is the total tax benefit compared to using a regular taxable account?

options.text: $924 — just the tax saved on the contribution
$1,920 — the combined savings from the deduction, tax-free growth, and tax-free withdrawal
$8,000 — the full withdrawal is a tax benefit
$3,850 — only the contribution amount is tax-advantaged

quiz_questions.explanation: Contribution deduction saves $3,850 x 0.24 = $924. Growth of $4,150 would have been taxed at ~24% = $996 in a taxable account. Total benefit = $924 + $996 = $1,920. The triple tax advantage saves on contributions, growth, and withdrawals.

quiz_questions.question_text: Greg is self-employed and earned $200,000. He wants to maximize retirement contributions. A Solo 401(k) allows $23,000 employee deferral plus 25% of net SE income as employer contribution. Approximately how much can he contribute total, and how much does this save at the 32% bracket?

options.text: $23,000 total; saves $7,360
Approximately $60,000 total; saves approximately $19,200
$46,000 total; saves $14,720
$69,000 total; saves $22,080

quiz_questions.explanation: Employee deferral: $23,000. Employer contribution: approximately 25% of net SE income (~$185,000 after SE tax deduction) = ~$37,000. Total ~$60,000. Tax savings = $60,000 x 0.32 = $19,200.

quiz_questions.question_text: Helen's income fluctuates: $150,000 in Year 1 (24% bracket) and $40,000 in Year 2 (12% bracket). She has a $15,000 year-end bonus she can receive in December (Year 1) or defer to January (Year 2). How much does deferring save?

options.text: $0 — timing does not matter

quiz_questions.explanation: In Year 1 at 24%: tax on $15,000 = $3,600. In Year 2 at 12%: tax on $15,000 = $1,800. Savings from deferring = $3,600 - $1,800 = $1,800. Income timing between years of different brackets is a powerful strategy.
